data.title: Nike's Loss Is On's Gain, Valuation Now Excessively Low (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
With extreme volatility reigning in the stock market, many high-quality businesses have sold off to fire-sale levels. Our best move as long-term-oriented investors is to apply careful stock selection to find attractive buying opportunities where valuation multiples have completely disconnected from fundamental reality.

On Holding (ONON), the Swiss shoemaker, is a perfect example of this. Since the start of the year, shares of On have shed ~30% of their value, putting the stock in a deep bear market. Losses picked up steam after the company's early March earnings release, when On's growth guidance for FY26 disappointed the markets.

I last wrote a buy article on On in March, after the company's Q4 earnings print. Since then, On has shed ~20% more value in a selloff that I believe has gone too far. I see a very opportunistic buying window here, and I'm upgrading the stock to a strong buy.

Leadership Shakeup Isn't a Risk

Let's start with the major news item that has caused On to plummet in recent sessions. In late March, On announced that its longtime CEO, Martin Hoffman, was retiring.

There's no doubt that Hoffman has been the driving force behind On in the past two decades. He served five years as CEO and the previous 13 years as CFO, overseeing the brand's evolution from a small startup into a global apparel heavyweight. At the same time, we see no concerns here because his replacements are On's co-founders, who will become the new co-CEOs: David Allemann and Caspar Coppetti.

The market is treating this change-up as bad news. I, however, don't see the return of the co-founders as highlighting any particular issues at On. In virtually any other company, a founder returning to lead a company would be read as a positive long-term commitment from major shareholders.

We think this is especially true as On is largely a consumer-driven company. This isn't a leadership change that will severely impact sales teams or scuttle enterprise deals in motion. I view this as more of a routine, planned retirement alongside the return of long-term-oriented founders who provide substantial continuity to the business.

Nike's Loss is On's Gain, Especially in China

The other major factor that has occurred since On's quarterly print is Nike's (NKE) quarterly update. Shares of Nike have tumbled sharply post-earnings, hitting multi-year lows and suffering a 30%+ decline since the start of January.

The major issue that investors have taken with Nike's recent results is the sharp deterioration of sales in China. As shown in the chart below, Nike's February quarter featured a -10% y/y decline (in constant currency) in the Greater China region. Meanwhile, the company's core footwear lineup (73% of sales in the region) also saw a -10% y/y FX-neutral decline.

Commentators have pounced on the fact that Nike is facing a plethora of homegrown rivals in China, like Anta Sports and Li Ning. And yet we also can't ignore the share gains that On is enjoying in the region. In On's most recent quarter, overall sales in Asia-Pacific rose at 85% y/y (in constant currency) to CHF 126.5 million, still only 17% of total sales. Nike doesn't split its geographies the same way, but we note that Greater China plus Asia Pacific & Latin America was 28% of Nike's sales.

In other words, Nike's losses are translating into On's gains. On's outgoing CEO, Martin Hoffman, specifically called out China as a major growth driver for the company and noted that sales during the Lunar New Year period (which On will report alongside Q1 results) were especially strong. Per Hoffman's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Asia Pacific delivered another exceptional quarter, further solidifying its role as a key growth driver for the brand. Net sales reached CHF 126.5 million increasing 70.8% reported and 85.1% at constant currency. We continue to see deep resonance and incredibly high demand across the entire region and in all channels. We saw outstanding results from our double eleven execution in China. Ranking top 5 on Tmall for footwear over $140 in December.

This momentum carried into a very strong Chinese New Year performance. with in-store traffic in China more than doubling relative to our baseline. During the holiday, we saw our highest productivity globally in two of our Hong Kong stores, and a stellar performance in our recently opened Shenzhen flagship, our largest retail store in China. This location is capturing a high share of GenZ consumers and delivering an over 20% apparel share."

On's share price erosion this year has basically matched Nike's, even though it's clear that On is still achieving tremendous growth while Nike's market share is floundering.

Valuation, Risks, and Key Takeaways

This brings me to the crux of my strong buy rating for On: its fundamental strength is completely disconnected from its sagging valuation multiples.

At current share prices near $33, On trades at a market cap of $10.93 billion. We net off the CHF 1.02 billion of cash against CHF 59.5 million of debt on the company's latest balance sheet (roughly CHF 960 million in net cash, or $1.20 billion in USD terms using the latest exchange rates) to arrive at an enterprise value of $9.73 billion.

For FY26, On is guiding to "at least" 23% y/y revenue growth to CHF 3.44 billion (at a minimum) and 18.5-19.0% adjusted EBITDA margins. Using midpoint adjusted EBITDA margins against minimum revenue gives us CHF 645 million in adjusted EBITDA, or $806.3 million.

This positions On's valuation at just 12.1x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. This is lower not just versus Nike (NKE) but against other core sportswear peers like Amer Sports (AS), the parent company of Salomon running shoes and Wilson Tennis.

Of course, there are certainly also risks we need to keep in mind here. Macroeconomic conditions could worsen, which could slow down On's sales progress. On is also still burdened by tariffs in the U.S., and there is plenty of uncertainty over the fate of tariffs in 2026 and beyond.

That said, these risks are fully priced in, and we welcome the opportunity to buy On at a low double-digit EBITDA multiple (when it so recently traded at >30x EBITDA last year). Buy here and stay long.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: Market Growth May Already Be Priced In (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Kinder Morgan, Inc. (KMI) is entering into another major growth cycle for natural gas in the U.S. underpinned by electric utility demand and the potential for accelerated LNG export growth over the next decade resulting from geopolitical disruptions in the Middle East. With one of the largest domestic pipeline systems, I believe KMI will play a critical role in supplying natural gas to facilitate U.S. reindustrialization and electrifying large-scale data centers. Despite the compelling growth story, I believe shares have priced in the growth opportunity ahead; I am recommending KMI shares with a Hold rating with a price target of 30.39/share at 11.67x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

Kinder Morgan Operational Update

KMI may be driving into the next capital growth cycle as domestic demand for natural gas is set to expand over the coming years to power industrialization and large-scale data center developments. With natural gas taking precedence as a critical source for load capacity, I believe that KMI may play a critical role in matching supply and demand through take-or-pay and fee-based arrangements with 4% unhedged capacity to realize commodity pricing dispersions. Accordingly, KMI has a $10b backlog and an additional $10b in natural gas project opportunities with the expectation of investing $3b in capital to deliver cash flow growth over the coming years. Driving demand is domestic power consumption growth and LNG export capacity expansions. As part of this, roughly $1.2b of the gas opportunities are tied to LNG projects.

Of the $10b in backlog, 60% is associated with power projects, which may in part be tied to data center capacity development. Taking into consideration the addressable market, utilities are expected to invest $1.1T through 2030 in grid modernization and new power generation infrastructure. Driving this matter, GE Vernova (GEV), a major manufacturer of industrial and aeroderivative gas turbines, has experienced substantial backlog growth in recent quarters, exiting Q4 ’25 with a gas turbine backlog of 83GW of capacity.

KMI began construction on the Trident Intrastate Pipeline project in Q1 ’26. The 2Bcf/d in capacity pipeline was designed to transport natural gas from West of Houston to the Golden Pass LNG terminal and industrial corridor near Port Arthur, Texas, expecting to bring phase I online in q1’27 and phase II in Q4 ’28.

Another major project in the works is the South System Expansion 4 Project, which is expected to add 1.3Bcf/d of gas capacity across the SNG South Mainline facilities from Clarke County, Mississippi, connecting other regions as part of the pipeline system. The project was designed to support grid capacity across the Southeastern region, expanding upon existing infrastructure. The project is expected to cost $3.5b with a proposed in-service date in Q4 ’28 for phase I and Q4 ’29 for phase II.

Also supporting gas demand in the Southeast region is the Mississippi Crossing project, which was designed to transport 2.1Bcf/d in additional capacity to shippers in the region. The project is forecast to cost $1.7b in capital with a proposed in-service date in Q4 ’28.

From a value perspective, KMI is relatively derisked in terms of cash flows with 96% of capacity being assigned to a fee-based or take-or-pay structure, providing the firm with stability and exceptionally appealing durability during a time of commodity price volatility.

In Q4 ’25, KMI realized a 19% increase in G&P volumes, primarily coming out of the Haynesville system. The Haynesville Basin is a strategic asset given its regional positioning near Gulf Coast LNG terminals, providing KMI with an appealing opportunity for growth as export capacity increases driven by international gas demand.

One factor that is emerging as a potential growth catalyst for U.S. LNG export capacity is the Iranian war. Prior to the war, Qatar accounted for 20% of the global LNG shipments, most of which were destined for European and Asian countries. With the war emerged a major supply chain disruption that began with the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and escalated to natural gas facilities and gas fields being targets for missile strikes, resulting in substantial damages that may take years to get back online. This has resulted in a growing dispersion between the price of domestic gas and international benchmarks, potentially creating a market environment that may enkindle further development of LNG export capacity in the U.S. Taking into consideration the theme of the matter, the longer the Iranian war wages on, the greater the potential dependency on U.S. gas the world may become. While this isn’t an ideal situation, this could create a growth opportunity for the U.S. gas market, particularly for midstream operators that may be responsible for transporting and marketing natural gas.

The EIA is currently forecasting U.S. LNG export capacity to more than double to 28.7Bcf/d, up from around 11.4Bcf/d in 2024. Given the state of the global energy economy and the regional risks in the Middle East, I suspect that there may be increased developments in the coming years to drive more capacity if European and Asian countries turn more heavily towards U.S. imports.

Whether the trade agreement still stands, the U.S. and Japan struck a deal in July 2025 for Japan to invest $550b in U.S. industries, including energy infrastructure tied to LNG export capacity and the grid. The European Union signed a similar agreement for $750b in US energy investments, largely tied to natural gas exports. Given the situation in the Middle East, these agreements may be emphasized to accelerate the development of domestic midstream assets to facilitate international demand for LNG cargoes, an appealing situation for KMI if this plays out.

Kinder Morgan Financial Position & Forecast

Looking ahead to Q1 ’26, I’m forecasting KMI to generate $4.7b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.39/share. My forecast is based on the assumption of increased productivity across gas basins, playing into KMI’s fee-based market. Looking further out, I believe KMI can realize accretive growth as gas demand picks up in the U.S. While the LNG theme is going to be more of a long-term strategy, I’m expecting domestic demand will drive the market over the coming years, delivering higher volumes of production in core regions KMI participates in.

Looking at the balance sheet, KMI closed Q4 ’25 with $109mm in cash & equivalent and $32b in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage ratio of 3.80x net debt/aEBITDA. KMI has done a good job at reducing its leverage exposure while maintaining its 3.5-4.5x range, providing the firm with the flexibility to reinvest in capital projects over the coming years.

Risks Related to Kinder Morgan

Bull Case

KMI may play a critical role in supplying natural gas to support regional developments of data centers and industrial facilities, with the expectation of domestic demand for natural gas to deliver growth over the coming years. KMI has a relatively low risk profile with its fee-based & take-or-pay approach, delivering to investors with stable cash flows rather than taking on unneeded commodity pricing risk.

Bear Case

KMI’s fee-based approach may limit the firm’s full upside potential from marketing natural gas to critical regions and customers, particularly when considering the expectation of demand picking up over the coming years.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

KMI shares are currently trading at a rich premium of 12.54x EV/aEBITDA, making shares relatively expensive when compared to their historical trading range. While the growth story is very compelling, I believe that growth may be priced in, limiting investors’ upside potential to premium expansion and dividend yield. That being said, a 3.55% dividend yield can be appealing, espeically when considering KMI's corporate structure.

Fundamentally, I believe shares should trade at 30.39/share at 11.67x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA; I am recommending KMI with a Hold rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Northrim BanCorp Is Still Worth Banking On
data.description:
In January of this year, one financial institution that I made the decision to reaffirm as a 'buy' candidate was none other than Northrim BanCorp (NRIM), a rather interesting player in the financial space that has done an exceptional job from an operational standpoint as of late. Revenue and profits have increased nicely and asset quality remains robust. The stock is rather pricey when it comes to its price to book and price to tangible book multiples. But relative to earnings, shares are cheap and the firm's net interest margin continues to expand. It is worth noting that there could be some negative impact on net interest margin moving forward as interest rates decline. But overall net profits can be expected to rise absent something big and unexpected occurring.

Since that article, the stock has unfortunately underperformed the market. While the S&P 500 is down 8.3%, shares of this prospect have dropped 18.8%. This is painful, but the good news is that, in the entire time that I have been bullish about the company, it has drastically outperformed the market. Since I originally called it a 'buy' in August of 2023, the stock has jumped 111.2%. That's well over double the 42.9% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. At some point, the company will be deserving of a downgrade. But in all honesty, I don't see that happening just yet. Management continues to benefit, at least in the near term, from declining interest rates. And while the company is far from perfect as you will see shortly, it does still justify a bullish outlook.

In the event that new data comes out that justifies a change in opinion, I will adjust my rating accordingly. The good news is that new data is just around the horizon. Although management has not revealed yet when earnings will be released next, on April 23rd of last year, the firm announced results for the first quarter of its 2025 fiscal year. So we will likely see results for the first quarter of 2026 come out around that time this year too. Analysts do expect a rise in revenue, but profitability is forecasted to fall. Do you think that investors would be wise to pay careful attention to these results?

The picture has gotten better

Normally when I analyze a bank, I like to start with the balance sheet. But today, I feel like doing something a little different. One thing that really struck me as a big positive about Northrim BanCorp is just how well it has been doing from a revenue and profit standpoint. This has been the result in large part of an expansion in its net interest margin. Take the final quarter of 2025 as an example. It was 4.70% on an annualized basis at that time. This is already well above what banks typically generate. Plus, it represented a move up from the 4.41% that the company saw a year earlier. For the year as a whole, the increase was even more impressive from 4.28% to 4.69%. In the data below, you can see changes in yield and cost regarding loans, interest bearing deposits, and securities. You can see that, over the last year, assets have gotten more valuable to the company while liabilities have gotten cheaper.

This might seem odd considering that you would expect a declining interest rate environment to be bad for banks. While that statement would be true of most financial institutions, it is not true of banks in the short run. A large chunk of liabilities, mostly deposits, happen to be variable in nature. And as interest rates are slashed, banks are able to immediately cut how much they are paying depositors without seeing a similar decline in assets. This does not mean that the institution is immune on the asset side. In fact, only 27.3% of its loans that were due more than one year out from the end of the final quarter ended up being fixed in nature. However, many of the variable rate loans that the institution has have interest rate floors to them. It is worth noting that while the net interest margin picture has been favorable for the company, management does expect some deterioration in it in the event that interest rates do change. But if we focus on the short term, overall net profits stand to rise almost no matter what.

In the images above and below, you can actually see two different scenarios of what would happen if interest rates change. The first of these images shows that a shock in interest rates would actually have a negative impact on net interest income for the company. But that is not what we have seen leading up to this point. Actual changes in net profits, however, are shown in the subsequent image. Because of a change in other operating income and expenses associated with the mortgage banking activities for the institution, we would actually expect overall profitability to rise over the course of a year if there is a sudden change in the interest rate environment, even if it is a substantial decline in what interest rates currently are. Of course, as the second image shows, the second year change in net income from the company's base scenario would be worse. But that's to be expected considering that at some point lower interest rates will affect the company as assets slowly adjust lower.

In the chart below, you can see the impact that higher net interest margin has had on the company from a revenue and profit standpoint. The overall picture has improved quite a bit if I do say so myself. That has been especially true on the operating side of the business. And as a result, net profits for the institution have risen. For 2025 as a whole, net income was $64.6 million. That was nearly double the $37 million reported the previous year.

Of course, we should not ignore changes in the balance sheet also. In the chart below, you can see key parts of the balance sheet, including deposits, loans, securities, cash and cash equivalents, and debt. At the end of 2024, the institution had deposits and loans of $2.68 billion and $2.11 billion, respectively. And by the end of 2025, these categories totaled $2.81 billion and $2.27 billion, respectively. All else being the same, a larger balance sheet translates to greater revenue and profits also. What's more, the company has kept its overall debt quite low at $81.7 million. If anything, the institution could afford to borrow more without materially changing its risk profile. However, it's likely that the company keeps debt in check because uninsured deposit exposure is quite high. At the end of 2025, the company had about $1.1 billion of uninsured deposits. That was about 38% of all deposits, which happens to be above the 30% maximum that I typically like to see.

When it comes to valuing the company, there are a couple of ways to do it. The first way is through the price to earnings approach. In the first chart below, you can see how the company stacks up against five other similar banks in this regard. On a price to earnings basis, it is cheaper than any of the competitors that I compared it to. It also happens to be cheaper, at a multiple of 7.8, than the 10 maximum I typically like to see. In the subsequent chart below that, you can see that the picture is different when it comes to the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In this case, we are looking at a firm that is trading quite high. However, I view that as only a slight negative considering the overall asset quality of the institution.

In the first chart below, you can see Northrim BanCorp's return on assets compared to the return on assets of the five institutions that I stacked it up against. The 1.50% reading that we get here is well above the 1% minimum that I look for. It also puts the company above three of the five institutions that I stacked it up against. The chart below that is even more impressive, with return on equity of 15.16% surpassing the return on equity ratings of four of the five competitors. It's also well above the 10% minimum that I look for in banks. It's also important to make note of the fact that non-performing assets and loans of the institution seemed to be in check. For context, I typically like to see no more than 0.50% of assets and loans classified as non-performing. But at the end of 2025, these readings were 0.35% and 0.49%, respectively.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, management will soon be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will come in at $50.6 million. That would be up nicely from the $45.5 million that the company reported a year earlier. However, profitability is another matter entirely. They expect earnings per share to plummet from $2.38 last year to just $0.55 this year. At first glance, this seems disastrous. But context is important here. Management conducted a four-for-one stock split in September of last year. So when you adjust for that, earnings per share last year would have been $0.60. This does mean, still, a decline, with net profits contracting from $13.3 million to $12.4 million. That's not disastrous on its own, but investors would be wise to look at the devil in the details if results do come in worse than anticipated. I will say that if profitability does decline, it will more likely than not be due to a larger provision in credit losses, but that is speculative in nature. Regardless of the decline, this could make shares look a bit more expensive relative to earnings, but they are still cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that the market continues to underestimate Northrim BanCorp. It is not, to be clear, the best investment opportunity in the world. But for those looking for a high quality institution at a good price, this certainly fits the bill. Changes in the interest rate environment will have an interesting impact on the institution moving forward. But as management has demonstrated, the company has been incredibly resilient in the near term. At the end of the day, I believe that it justifies a soft 'buy' rating at this time.

data.title: Delta Air Lines: Why It Is A Relative Winner (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Back in early March, I stated that, in the airline sector, "I would be much more comfortable holding United Airlines (UAL), Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), and International Consolidated Airlines (ICAGY) [...] than American Airlines (AAL), Southwest Airlines (LUV), and even US Global Jets (JETS) itself." While I have written about nearly all the tickers listed here, I skipped a discussion on Delta, which is what I will do today.

The Atlanta-based airline is one of my favorite companies and stocks to own in this volatile and cyclical sector, as it fits the mold of my current industry-wide thesis: quality will likely outperform more speculative, leveraged (operational and financial) plays—possibly in the short- to medium-term, but even more likely over the long haul.

Thesis, Risks, And The Track Record

As I explained before, the airline space seemed to be in a decent place as of early March. While I am fairly confident that we are not currently living through the early stages of cyclical economic expansion, which would be a positive for the sector at large and for more speculative plays specifically, we are (or at least were, prior to the Middle East crisis and the federal budget-driven disruptions to TSA and airport operations) in fairly stable "cruising altitude", economically speaking.

Airline stock prices are naturally quite sensitive to, among a few other important factors, future revenue growth. And per my previous analysis:

Airline revenues are somewhat reliably predicted by a few leading indicators of economic activity, in the following order of importance: jobless claims (a reflection of the strength in the US job market), consumer expectations of future business conditions (a barometer of travelers' confidence in what lies ahead for the economy), and building permits (likely more of a broad gauge of economic strength rather than something travel-specific).

The chart below depicts how these economic KPIs have done a very good job at anticipating actual revenue growth reported, with a lead of about 12 months, going back all the way to the 1990s.

True, all the above leading indicators may have taken a turn for the worse in the last handful of weeks, and maybe we have yet to see the deteriorating numbers printed on a report. Generally, this is a key risk for the airline sector at large, including for DAL. But if this is the case, my "buy quality in the airline space" thesis actually gains strength, in my view, on a relative basis—think of a long-short strategy in which quality is bought and speculation is sold.

Speaking of long-short, I plugged the numbers for the month of March, calling DAL, UAL, and ICAGY "quality" (buy) and AAL, LUV, and JETS "speculation" (sell), with equal weighting of +1/3 on the longs and -1/3 on the shorts. The chart and table below show how the approach would have performed against the S&P 500 (SPY) through the TSA slowdowns, the Middle East crisis, and the ensuing crude oil price shock.

One month of lavish outperformance is not enough to warrant a victory lap. But it does help to illustrate what investing in quality might look like during periods of market turbulence.

What is particularly interesting, however, is the correlation coefficient between the long-short play and the S&P 500 of virtually zero. It suggests that the recent outperformance of my three favorite airline tickers might not be merely explained by investors desperately seeking safer havens but rather a certain antifragile nature of these higher-quality stocks (i.e., relatively more resilience when it matters most).

A Look At Delta's Fundamentals

Depending on what metric I choose to focus on, Delta could be considered the highest-quality of the US airlines today. The company has historically been one of the leaders in operational efficiency, producing the highest per-unit margin before COVID-19 (see chart below). I believe this is a function of more competitive routes and a hub advantage over its peers that allows the company to keep average prices high relative to average operating costs.

Things have changed a bit in the post-pandemic environment, primarily due to (1) rising personnel costs, as the airlines had to ramp up capacity quickly starting in 2022, and (2) a wonderful job done by United Airlines through its United Next restructuring program, which has allowed the company to steal the badge of leader in per-unit operating margin in the US away from Delta.

Delta also enjoys a favorable balance sheet position relative to its peers. The company's ratio of net debt and leases to assets has declined in the past five years to only 20% at the end of 2025, threatening Southwest's title of least financially leveraged. Compare this number to American's much less enticing 48%, which was barely an improvement at all from the pre-COVID period.

This reduction in net debt is quite a feat, in my opinion. Delta was able to pull it off despite a decline in per-unit margin, as explained above (i.e., strong pricing environment but rapidly rising operating costs due to capacity ramp-up). Supporting lavish cash flow production was Delta's ability to return capacity and occupancy to its pre-COVID levels fairly quickly and competently.

One of the most likely pushbacks that I expect to hear from skeptics is Delta's valuation. As the chart below depicts, the gap between DAL's forward P/E and that of UAL is the widest that it has been in the past 12 months, at over two turns. The March 2026 market route, in fact, only impacted DAL to the extent that it increased volatility, as the share price is actually higher today than it was in the first couple of days of last month.

But I still believe that DAL trades at its current valuation levels for a good reason. Sure, were the multiple today the same as it was a year ago, I might consider the stock a pound-the-table buy at a forward P/E of less than 7.5x, which is not the case. But at least for now, until or unless valuations expand further, I still choose to keep Delta shares in my "likely winners" column.

Upgrading From Neutral To (Relative) Bullish

In my most recent DAL article, published about one year ago, I downgraded the stock from buy to neutral. My more bearish views, however, were absolute and not relative—i.e., I feared for the airline space in general and not for Delta specifically, relative to its peer group, as evidenced in the paragraph below:

The more sophisticated investor may have the willingness and ability to set up a long-short trade that favors a group of airlines over others. Ordinarily, I would consider DAL a strong candidate for a buy in such a setup due to the company's superior fundamentals.

Today, due to my renewed focus on winners vs. losers in the airline space, I walk my downgrade back. Said differently, I continue to have concerns over the health of the airline space in the short term, as laid out in my sector-wide thesis above. But relative to the likes of Southwest and American, I think that Delta will outperform due to it being fundamentally superior, despite richer valuation multiples.

My view is ultimately in line with Seeking Alpha's quant rating. According to it, Delta stands out in growth, profitability (which I appreciate most), and momentum, with each of these three factors receiving a rating of B+ or better. Valuation is listed as a B, per the quant framework, which I see as less than concerning.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
